text: The surgeon performs dissection to expose the surgical field, leaving the vessels behind while mobilizing the pancreas and tumor upwards in preparation for stapling.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon and splenic flexure, revealing the inferior edge of the pancreas. The lesser sac is entered near the hilus of the spleen to improve visibility by preventing dangling fat from obscuring the view.
The surgical field is fully exposed, revealing the anterior surface of the pancreas. A growing cystic neoplasm is visible on the pancreas.

The surgeon mobilizes and identifies the inferior edge of the pancreas, revealing the splenic vein posteriorly. The superior surface of the pancreas is exposed, with the splenic artery visible and being dissected. The dissection progresses towards the tail, separating the splenic vein from the pancreas parenchyma along the superior edge.
The surgeon identifies the tip of the tail of the pancreas and begins to flip it upwards, carefully separating it from the adjacent vessels.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to sever small branches off the splenic vein, creating a window to lift the tip of the tail of the pancreas away from the splenic artery and vein. The attachments connecting the splenic artery to the pancreas tail are also being cut with the ultrasonic shears.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas from the splenic artery and vein using a lateral to medial approach. The cautery is used to detach most attachments, while smaller branches are severed with ultrasonic shears.
The pancreas is visibly mobile, with the surgical field showing the proximal area to the cystic lesion. The surgeon introduces a blue load endoscopic stapler to prepare for stapling the pancreas.

The video shows a clean staple line on the pancreas, indicating successful closure of the gland. The splenic artery and vein appear intact, with no visible damage. The spleen is well-perfused, although there is slight ischemia observed at the inferior pole.
